output.summary_final:
{
    "title": "Building Trails and Skills That Last",
    "bullets": [
        "Effective trail building prioritizes good drainage to maintain rideability and prevent soil erosion and ruts.",
        "Riding speed is gained through earlier, smoother braking rather than taking bigger risks.",
        "Proper berm technique involves looking through the exit, keeping hips centered, and building pressure gradually.",
        "Bike maintenance should be a simple, routine check of bolts, chain wear, stanchions, and brake pads.",
        "Mysterious bike creaks are often resolved by cleaning contact surfaces, re-greasing threads, and re-torquing to specification.",
        "Tire pressure and casing selection typically have a greater impact on performance than minor suspension adjustments.",
        "Skill improvement is best achieved by repeating a trail segment while changing only one variable at a time.",
        "In wet conditions, good decision-making to protect traction and trails is the most valuable skill."
    ]
}

{"title": "On-Call That Doesn't Break People", "bullets": ["A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare and the response is obvious when it happens.", "Alerting should be action-oriented; if no one can take immediate action, it probably shouldn't page.", "Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership.", "Secrets handling improves when tokens are short-lived, scoped narrowly, and rotated with automation.", "Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning.", "Writing things down turns disagreement into collaboration by pointing at assumptions instead of people.", "Architecture decisions are mostly tradeoffs: cost versus reliability, speed versus correctness, autonomy versus consistency.", "Security is strongest when the secure path is the easiest path, not enforced by shame or heroics."]}

{
    "title": "Wreck Diving Fundamentals",
    "bullets": [
        "Calmness is critical for safety as it slows breathing and enables deliberate decision-making.",
        "For navigation, always use a primary reference and a backup, such as heading plus depth or reef contour plus current direction.",
        "The ability to call off a dive early without ego is one of the most valuable skills a diver can have.",
        "Carrying a backup light provides mental security and options, going beyond mere equipment redundancy.",
        "Good buoyancy control protects the environment and reduces task load for navigation and photography.",
        "Practice neutral buoyancy drills like hovering to make handling choppy conditions easier.",
        "Most underwater stress stems from surprise and lack of rehearsal, not the inherent difficulty of a problem.",
        "Effective dive plans are simple, shared with the team, and include clear turn pressures and exit routes."
    ]
}

{
    "title": "Underwater Photography: Lighting and Workflow Essentials",
    "bullets": [
        "Underwater photography success depends more on lighting and positioning than on camera gear.",
        "A small strobe angle change reduces backscatter by moving the light beam away from the lens axis.",
        "Lighting is about controlling shadows, spill, and the viewer's focus; use grids to contain light and keep areas dark.",
        "In mixed lighting, decide on a dominant color temperature and correct all other sources relative to it.",
        "Practice with a single light teaches placement better than a complicated kit by forcing focused learning.",
        "Maintain routines like labeling files and writing captions the same day to prevent memory gaps in long-term projects.",
        "Great images come from a clear idea and a simple, consistently executed setup.",
        "If you cannot explain why a light is needed, you probably do not need it yet."
    ]
}

{
    "title": "Index Investing Without the Myths",
    "bullets": [
        "Index investing works by reducing costs and decision errors, not by predicting the future.",
        "True risk is the chance of permanent loss or failing to meet goals, not just volatility.",
        "Use position sizing rules to prevent any single idea from causing a portfolio-ending mistake.",
        "Lowering fees compounds quietly and effectively increases returns without taking on more risk.",
        "Behavioral discipline is critical: stay consistent despite loud headlines and portfolio drawdowns.",
        "Effective diversification requires assets with different underlying drivers, not just different tickers.",
        "Conduct stress tests for investments like real estate to assess resilience to higher rates, vacancies, and repairs.",
        "Drawdowns involve both math and emotion; a 50% loss requires a 100% gain to recover, altering timelines."
    ]
}
